title: Segfault from calling `StringIO` methods in threads on free-threading debug build
author: devdanzin
body:
# Crash report

### What happened?

It's possible to segfault or abort the interpreter in a free-threading debug build by calling StringIO methods from threads:
```python
from io import StringIO
from threading import Thread

for _ in range(10):
    alive = []

    sio = StringIO(newline="")
    def call_stringio_methods():
        try:
            sio.write("\x00" * 10 ** 5)
            sio.seek(0)  # Comment this line out for a different segfault
            sio.readlines()
        except Exception:
            pass

    for _ in range(5):
        alive.append(Thread(target=call_stringio_methods))
    for t in alive:
        t.start()
```

The segfault for this MRE can happen in different places, all seemingly related to `_stringio_readline`. If you comment out the `sio.seek(0)` line, a different segfault happens.

Found using [fusil](https://github.com/devdanzin/fusil) by @vstinner.


### CPython versions tested on:

CPython main branch

### Operating systems tested on:

Linux

### Output from running 'python -VV' on the command line:

Python 3.15.0a0 experimental free-threading build (heads/main:ac7511062bf, Jun  9 2025, 15:09:10) [GCC 11.4.0]

<!-- gh-linked-prs -->
### Linked PRs
* gh-135412
* gh-135425
* gh-135430
* gh-135439
* gh-135449
<!-- /gh-linked-prs -->

comments.author: kumaraditya303
comments.body: This seems very similar to https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/134908 we should add critical section around iteration

comments.author: ZeroIntensity
comments.body: We broke the refleak buildbots.

comments.author: corona10
comments.body: I plan to take a look at this issue deeply from today cc @ZeroIntensity @vstinner @colesbury

comments.author: vstinner
comments.body: What's the status of this issue?

comments.author: ZeroIntensity
comments.body: The concern is that in the process of making `StringIO` memory-safe, we also made it thread-safe, but the pure-Python implementation is only memory-safe. It didn't seem right to have that much of a difference between the two implementations.

comments.author: vstinner
comments.body: Is there a way to make _pyio.StringIO thread-safe?

comments.author: ZeroIntensity
comments.body: Yeah, we could add locks to it. But is that a good idea? Should we guarantee that `StringIO` is thread-safe?

comments.author: cmaloney
comments.body:
It looks like [`_pyio.StringIO`](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/3.14/Lib/_pyio.py#L2702-L2750) is implemented on top of `_pyio.TextIOWrapper` containing a `_pyio.BytesIO`. `TextIOWrapper` and `BytesIO` (_io/C PR: GH-132616) in `_io` are expected to be thread-safe so I'd argue it's reasonable to have `StringIO` be. How far `_pyio` is from implementing that I'm not sure (in the PRs I've looked at only changed and added tests to `_io`)

Personally I'm okay with just `_io` being thread-safe for now; more thread safety in these I think is worthwhile for the cost and a little step for just `_io` is better than no step for to me.

comments.author: corona10
comments.body:
@vstinner @ZeroIntensity @cmaloney 
Sorry for the delayed response. I’ve been tied up with several issues.

Since PEP 779 has been accepted with a number of considerations:
https://discuss.python.org/t/pep-779-criteria-for-supported-status-for-free-threaded-python/84319/123

In any case, we should update the relevant documentation to clarify whether thread-safety is guaranteed or not.
Additionally, please keep in mind the performance trade-offs, as there is a performance guardrail that allows for no more than a 10% regression in overall performance.

If making a specific object thread-safe causes a significant performance regression, then please consider ensuring only memory-safety for that object and explicitly documenting that thread-safety is not guaranteed.

comments.author: kumaraditya303
comments.body:
> If making a specific object thread-safe causes a significant performance regression, then please consider ensuring only memory-safety for that object and explicitly documenting that thread-safety is not guaranteed.

I don't think there were concerns about significant perf loss in this case, and the easiest way to ensure memory safety is to add critical section which means we should just add back the critical section which was reverted.

comments.author: corona10
comments.body: @kumaraditya303 Let me follow up this issue from this week, I was too busy with other issues for several weeks.

comments.author: corona10
comments.body: @kumaraditya303 If we decide to make C implementation to be thread-safe. We must make a pure Python implementation to be thread-safe too (currently not). That's what we require from PEP 779. And pure python implementation performance will not be a big issue.

comments.author: colesbury
comments.body: @corona10 - we've never required that. Historically, there are lots of cases where the C implementation is thread-safe and the the Python implementation is not. Off the top of my head, this is true for both `OrderedDict` and `abc`.

comments.author: corona10
comments.body:
> Documentation expectations
> Documentation must be clearly written and maintained.
> For Python users: What guarantees exist, and how are they affected by the free-threaded build on all APIs in all modules of the standard library.

Approval of PEP 779 requires the recording the status of thread-safety at the docs.python.org
So then, Python users only rely on the C implementation from now on? At least from the current fix should guarantee the status of thread safe.

comments.author: corona10
comments.body: My simple question would be if we do not guarantee both implementations into the same implementation, then how will we record it on docs.python.org?

comments.author: colesbury
comments.body: The docs are for [`io.StringIO`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/io.html#io.StringIO). We should document that behavior. `_pyio` is, as far as I can tell, undocumented.

comments.author: corona10
comments.body:
I am not sure why you separate the fallback implementation, io.StringIO should be documented as to whether it is thread-safe or not, if we consider the next step and this is very important.
 (IIUC it includes a fallback implementation)
Similar concerns were issued with other PR https://github.com/python/cpython/pull/137021#issuecomment-3107280111 

I am fine with making a pure Python implementation slower (I need to find, but I approved at least one PR with that change.)

comments.author: corona10
comments.body: Here is the PR that I approved for the same condition. https://github.com/python/cpython/pull/136218#pullrequestreview-2985217259

comments.author: ZeroIntensity
comments.body: I think most modules that have been made thread-safe under FT do not have a safe pure-Python implementation.

comments.author: colesbury
comments.body:
> I am not sure why you separate the fallback implementation

It's not a "fallback" in the normal sense of the word. The only way to get `_pyio` is to explicitly the import the undocumented `_pyio` module. `import io` never gives you `_pyio`. The C extension isn't optional and `io.py` only uses the C extension:

https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/525784aa65d35a5609aba53c873a9a3a578f992b/Lib/io.py#L53

> Similar concerns were issued with other PR https://github.com/python/cpython/pull/137021#issuecomment-3107280111

I don't think this is a legitimate concern. This is not new behavior. It's long been the case -- since `_pyio` was split from `io` in 3.1 -- that `io.StringIO` is thread-safe and `_pyio.StringIO` is not. That's not currently true in the FT build and we need to fix that. We shouldn't expand scope to `_pyio.StringIO`.

comments.author: corona10
comments.body:
> that io.StringIO is thread-safe and _pyio.StringIO is not

I don't get it, it was thread-safe because GIL exists? It's not? 
I think that this is why https://docs.python.org/3/library/io.html#multi-threading we have not recorded it as thread-safe even in the default build. One thing we have to do from now on is write this status explicitly, even if it has not been documented.

> It's not a "fallback" in the normal sense of the word. The only way to get _pyio is to explicitly the import the undocumented _pyio module. import io never gives you _pyio. The C extension isn't optional and io.py only uses the C extension:

Okay, but it still needs that we should record the thread-safe status somewhere in docs.python.org
Then, can we document that the FT build does not guarantee the thread safety of _pyio implementation? (Maybe just top of the  io page)

comments.author: corona10
comments.body:
> I think most modules that have been made thread-safe under FT do not have a safe pure-Python implementation.

Yeah, that's one issue that I have tackled recently. If the fallback implementation exists, then we need to make them have the same behavior... because it's related to the documentation issue.

comments.author: corona10
comments.body: The easiest solution would be removing pure Python implementation from FT build 😆

comments.author: vstinner
comments.body:
> The easiest solution would be removing pure Python implementation from FT build 😆

You might add a sentence in _pyio classes docstrings to mention that they are not thread-safe.

comments.author: vstinner
comments.body: So what's the io.StringIO and _pyio.StringIO status at the end?
